facts.fact: Thread creation costs 10-100 microseconds; process creation costs 1-10 milliseconds
Context switches between threads in the same process avoid TLB flushes, making thread switches 2-5x faster than process switches
Virtual address translation with TLB caching achieves >99% hit rates in practice, converting 5+ memory accesses into 1
A TLB miss requires a page walk with several memory accesses, costing more than an instruction or data cache miss
Linux soft limit for open file descriptors can be checked with 'ulimit -n' and ranges from 1024 (default) to 2048+ for tuned systems
System-wide file descriptor limit is defined by fs.file-max, checked via 'cat /proc/sys/fs/file-max', typically around 100K-200K on commodity systems
Async I/O with event loops (100 tasks) completes in <0.6 seconds with CPU <15% and flat memory; synchronous 100×200ms = 20 seconds with 100% CPU wait
Thread-per-request model wastes CPU idle cycles while threads block on I/O; at 1,000 concurrent requests, response time > 3 minutes
Kubernetes sends SIGTERM with default grace period of 30 seconds before forcing SIGKILL; SIGKILL cannot be caught
SIGTERM is catchable and allows cleanup; SIGINT is sent on Ctrl+C from terminal
Minor (soft) page faults average 1.27 microseconds (2552 CPU cycles) with tail latency of 3.20 microseconds
Major (hard) page faults from SSD cost ~30 microseconds (30× slower than memory access); from HDD cost ~8 milliseconds
x86 systems have minimum page fault cost of 1050 CPU cycles
Heap allocation adds overhead for segment headers and padding; memory pools (arena allocation) reduce internal fragmentation by amortizing overhead
io_uring achieves P50: 0.2ms vs epoll's 0.4ms, P99: 0.9ms vs epoll's 1.8ms in ping-pong workloads
epoll is efficient on Linux 2.6+; kqueue on FreeBSD 4.1+, OpenBSD 2.9+, NetBSD 2.0, macOS
Nginx thread pools improved peak p99 TTFB by 6× when handling disk-bound operations via non-blocking open/read
Zombie process occurs when child exits but parent hasn't called wait()/waitpid() to reap exit status
SIGCHLD handler must loop to reap all exited children; only one SIGCHLD is queued regardless of how many children exit
Setting SIG_IGN for SIGCHLD or using SA_NOCLDWAIT flag prevents zombies by discarding child exit status
Stack memory is LIFO and freed automatically on function return; heap requires explicit deallocation or GC
Stack allocation is faster (single reference adjustment); heap allocation is slower (find free block, manage fragmentation)
Each process or thread requires 1-8 MB of stack space; C10K problem (10,000 concurrent connections) exposed this limit
WhatsApp handled 2 million concurrent connections with 24 cores using Erlang on FreeBSD
MigratoryData served 10-12 million concurrent connections with 12 cores using Java on Linux
Async-signal-safe functions must not call malloc, free, printf, pthread_mutex_lock or perform dynamic symbol resolution
Multi-level page tables reduce memory overhead by not allocating space for unmapped address regions
Processes communicate via Pipes, Sockets, or Message Queues requiring kernel mediation; threads use shared heap directly
Process isolation prevents single-process crashes from affecting others; thread crashes within a process affect all threads

examples.scenario: Nginx deployment with non-blocking open/read in thread pools
examples.outcome: 6× improvement in peak p99 TTFB (time to first byte) for disk-bound requests

examples.scenario: Real-time messaging platform handling concurrent connections
examples.outcome: 2 million concurrent connections on 24 cores using Erlang on FreeBSD
10-12 million concurrent connections with 12 cores on Java/Linux

examples.scenario: File transfer server handling concurrent clients
examples.outcome: 10,000 concurrent clients over 1 Gbps Ethernet, defining the C10K problem

examples.scenario: Web framework handling 10,000 concurrent real-time connections
examples.outcome: Possible via epoll + async handlers; impossible with thread-per-request

analogies.analogy: Processes are like separate houses with independent utilities (memory, file descriptors); threads are like rooms in a house sharing utilities (heap, code). A broken room (thread) can leak into shared spaces.

Virtual addresses are like apartment numbers; physical addresses are like actual building locations. The TLB is a doorman who remembers frequent apartment-to-location mappings, avoiding the need to consult the master directory (page table) each time.
File descriptors are like open doors in a building. Too many open doors = cannot open new ones. ulimit -n is setting the max doors per tenant; fs.file-max is the total building capacity.

Thread-per-request is like hiring a worker for each customer; they stand idle if the customer is waiting on something. Async I/O is like one worker juggling multiple customers, moving to the next while waiting.
Minor page faults are like opening a file already in RAM (microseconds); major page faults are like having to fetch from backup storage (milliseconds). A cache miss.
SIGTERM is asking the restaurant to finish serving current customers and close the doors; SIGKILL is calling the fire marshal to empty the building immediately.

key_numbers.context: Same address space avoids TLB flush

key_numbers.metric: Virtual address translation speedup with TLB
key_numbers.context: Without TLB, each translation requires page table walk

key_numbers.metric: Default soft ulimit for file descriptors
key_numbers.context: Per process on most Linux distributions

key_numbers.metric: Async I/O event loop latency (100 tasks)

key_numbers.context: ~30× slower than memory access at ~1 microsecond
Kernel overhead regardless of page source
Maximum tasks waiting before rejecting new ones
Using non-blocking open/read in Nginx thread pools

common_misconceptions.misconception: Threads are always lighter than processes and always faster
common_misconceptions.reality: Threads are lighter to create, but process isolation prevents cascading failures. Choice depends on isolation needs and failure domain.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Virtual memory is just about swapping to disk
common_misconceptions.reality: Virtual memory is primarily for isolation, security, and memory management. Swapping to disk is a last-resort mechanism.

common_misconceptions.misconception: TLB misses are rare and negligible
common_misconceptions.reality: TLB misses require expensive page walks (multiple memory accesses) and are critical to performance tuning.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Increasing ulimit -n to 65536 is always safe
common_misconceptions.reality: Each connection consumes memory and kernel resources; system-wide fs.file-max and memory must support it.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Async I/O is always faster than threads
common_misconceptions.reality: Async I/O excels at I/O-bound concurrency. CPU-bound tasks are simpler and often faster with synchronous code.

common_misconceptions.misconception: SIGKILL is the 'normal' way to stop a process
common_misconceptions.reality: SIGKILL forces immediate termination without cleanup. SIGTERM is the polite, graceful alternative.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Page faults always stall the CPU for milliseconds
common_misconceptions.reality: Minor page faults cost microseconds; major page faults (disk I/O) cost milliseconds.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Heap allocation is slow; stack allocation is always fast
common_misconceptions.reality: Stack allocation is faster, but heap allows dynamic sizes and sharing. Stack overflow is also a risk.
External fragmentation means available memory is scattered; internal fragmentation means allocated blocks are larger than needed.

common_misconceptions.misconception: All functions are safe to call from signal handlers
common_misconceptions.reality: Only async-signal-safe functions are safe; printf, malloc, pthread_mutex_lock are NOT safe.
Zombies persist until parent calls wait()/waitpid() or parent exits; they occupy PID slots.

common_misconceptions.misconception: One SIGCHLD handler call reaps one child
common_misconceptions.reality: Only one SIGCHLD is queued regardless of how many children exit; handler must loop to reap all.
